perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information may be disclosed either visually, orally or in writing. Written material shall be identified and labeled "Confidential" or "Proprietary" to discloser. Verbal or visual information should be identified as "Confidential Information" when disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information may be disclosed either visually, orally or in writing. Written material shall be identified and labeled "Confidential" at the Disclosing Party's discretion. Verbal or visual information should be identified as "Confidential Information" only if the Receiving Party requests it.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract mandates labeling confidential information. The modified version makes it discretionary or conditional, which weakens the protection and creates uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets when reasonable efforts are made to maintain their secrecy. Making the labeling of confidential information discretionary weakens those efforts, potentially voiding trade secret protection under CUTSA, as it fails to demonstrate a reasonable effort to maintain secrecy. The act requires active steps to safeguard confidential information.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the original contract's requirement for labeling confidential information, making it discretionary or conditional. This weakens the efforts to maintain secrecy. The Law Explanation indicates that CUTSA protects trade secrets when reasonable efforts are made to maintain their secrecy. The change in the contract language weakens those efforts. Snippet 1 states that the title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Because the change undermines efforts to protect confidentiality, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Except as provided in Section 8(b) below, the parties agree that any dispute or controversy arising out of or relating to any interpretation, construction, performance or breach of this Agreement shall be settled by arbitration to be held in San Diego County, California in accordance with the Commercial Arbitration Rules then in effect of the American Arbitration Association.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that any dispute or controversy arising out of or relating to any interpretation, construction, performance or breach of this Agreement shall be settled first through direct negotiation. If negotiation fails, then the dispute will be settled by binding arbitration outside of California, without access to discovery.
perturbation.explanation: The change removes California arbitration rules and location, and limits discovery. This can be problematic if California law governs the underlying contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law favors arbitration within the state and with reasonable discovery. By mandating arbitration outside California and restricting discovery, the modified text contradicts the spirit and procedural aspects of the California Arbitration Act, potentially rendering the arbitration clause unenforceable in a California court.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1280 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 3. OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1280 - 1280.2] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1280. As used in this title: (a) “Agreement” includes, but is not limited to, agreements providing for valuations, appraisals, and similar proceedings and agreements between employers and employees or between their respective representatives. (b) “Award” includes, but is not limited to, an award made pursuant to an agreement not in writing. (c) “Consumer” means an individual who seeks, uses, or acquires, by purchase or lease, any goods or services for personal, family, or household purposes. (d) “Controversy” means any question arising between parties to an agreement whether the question is one of law or of fact or both. (e) “Drafting party” means the company or business that included a predispute arbitration provision in a contract with a consumer or employee. The term includes any third party relying upon, or otherwise subject to the arbitration provision, other than the employee or consumer. (f) “Employee” means any current employee, former employee, or applicant for employme
perturbation.justification: The change in the contract moves arbitration outside of California and restricts discovery. According to the Law Explanation, California law prefers arbitration within the state and with reasonable discovery. The changed text, therefore, contradicts California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 1280, defines key terms related to arbitration agreements within California law, providing a foundation for the argument that the original agreement was designed to comply with California's arbitration framework. By explicitly moving arbitration outside California and restricting discovery, the revised agreement departs from the presumed legal context established by the original agreement.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by the laws of the state most favorable to the prevailing party, as determined by the arbitrator.
perturbation.explanation: This introduces uncertainty and potential conflict by removing the specified governing law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law allows parties to choose a governing law, but generally respects that choice if it has a reasonable relationship to the contract. The modified clause introduces ambiguity and allows for a potentially arbitrary selection of law that may not bear any relation to the contract or the parties involved. This contradicts the established principles of choice of law in California contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies California law, providing certainty. The changed text replaces this with 'the state most favorable to the prevailing party,' creating ambiguity and potentially selecting a law unrelated to the contract or parties. This undermines the principle of agreed-upon governing law. While California Code, CIV 1646 states a contract should be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed, or where it is made if no place of performance is indicated, the modified text introduces an arbitrary selection process, deviating from the established practice of choosing a specific jurisdiction's law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall expire two (2) years from the Effective Date, but may be terminated prior to expiration by either party giving thirty (30) days' prior written notice to the other party; provided, however, the obligations to protect the Confidential Information in accordance with this Agreement shall survive for a period of five (5) years from the date of the last disclosure of Confidential Information is made under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall continue indefinitely until either party, at their sole discretion, decides to terminate it. Confidentiality obligations will cease immediately upon termination, regardless of prior disclosures.
perturbation.explanation: The change eliminates a defined term and removes protections for confidential information post-termination, even for previously disclosed material.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Eliminating the continuing obligation to protect confidential information upon termination directly contradicts the principles of CUTSA. A trade secret can be misappropriated even after a contract ends if the information remains confidential and valuable. Removing this protection weakens CUTSA's purpose.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract eliminates the obligation to protect confidential information after termination, directly conflicting with CUTSA. The original text ensured confidentiality for five years post-disclosure. The changed text, however, states confidentiality obligations cease immediately upon termination. The Law Explanation states that eliminating this continuing obligation contradicts CUTSA's principles, as trade secrets can be misappropriated even after contract termination if they remain confidential and valuable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates the existence of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) in California (CIV 3426), which implies legal protection for trade secrets. The changed text weakens these protections, because it removes the clause preventing the use of confidential information after the contract is terminated.

perturbation.original_text: All headings used herein are intended for reference purposes only and shall not affect the interpretation, or validity of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: All headings used herein shall be considered legally binding and will dictate the interpretation of the sections they precede.
perturbation.explanation: This reverses the standard legal understanding that headings are for convenience and not substantive parts of a contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally holds that the substance of a contract, not the headings, governs its interpretation. While headings can provide context, they are not typically considered binding terms. By making headings legally binding and dictating interpretation, the modified clause contradicts established principles of contract interpretation in California, potentially leading to interpretations contrary to the actual intent of the parties expressed in the body of the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1651 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1651. Where a contract is partly written and partly printed, or where part of it is written or printed under the special directions of the parties, and with a special view to their intention, and the remainder is copied from a form originally prepared without special reference to the particular parties and the particular contract in question, the written parts control the printed parts, and the parts which are purely original control those which are copied from a form. And if the two are absolutely repugnant, the latter must be so far disregarded. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the typical legal understanding that headings are for reference and do not dictate the substance of a contract. The original text stated that headings are for reference only and do not affect interpretation or validity. The changed text reverses this, making headings legally binding and dictating interpretation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Code, CIV 1651, discusses the interpretation of contracts, prioritizing written parts over printed or form-copied parts when there is a conflict. While it doesn't directly address headings, it establishes a hierarchy of interpretation where the specific intent (written parts) outweighs standardized elements (printed parts). By making headings legally binding, the modified clause elevates a non-substantive element to a position of authority, potentially overriding the actual written content of the contract, thereby contradicting general principles of contract interpretation as outlined in the law explanation.
